children.name: Vanga
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Hook-billed Vanga, the namesake of the vanga family, endemic to Madagascar. It has a thick, strongly hooked bill that is distinctly crossed at the tip, an adaptation for extracting prey from tree crevices. It is a medium-sized, boldly patterned bird with a black-and-white plumage that makes it easily identifiable in the Malagasy forests.

children.name: Vanga curvirostris
children.description: A striking black-and-white bird with a large, hooked bill that is crossed at the very tip, endemic to the forests and woodlands of Madagascar. It uses its unique bill to extract prey from under bark and from leaf axils, foraging methodically along branches. Its loud, ringing calls are a characteristic sound of eastern Malagasy forests.

children.name: Artamella
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga endemic to Madagascar, containing the White-headed Vanga. It has a striking white head contrasting with black wings and tail, and a relatively slender, straight bill adapted for gleaning insects from foliage. It is a social species often seen in small groups.

children.name: Artamella viridis
children.description: A distinctive vanga with a pure white head, black upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the dry deciduous forests of western and northern Madagascar. It is a social and active forager, often moving through the canopy in small, noisy groups. Its calls include loud, nasal whistles and chattering notes.

children.name: Leptopterus
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga containing the Chabert Vanga, a small, pale-eyed species endemic to Madagascar. It has a relatively slender, straight bill and is an active canopy insectivore, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its pale blue-grey plumage makes it one of the more subtly coloured vangas.

children.name: Leptopterus chabert
children.description: A small, pale blue-grey vanga with a white belly and a distinctive pale blue eye, found in forests and woodlands across Madagascar. It is an active, acrobatic forager that gleans insects from leaves and branches in the canopy, often in mixed-species flocks. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Mystacornis
children.description: A monotypic genus of terrestrial vanga endemic to Madagascar, containing Crossley's Vanga. It has a long, slender, slightly decurved bill and strong legs, adapted for a ground-foraging lifestyle in the leaf litter of the forest floor. It is one of the most secretive and poorly known members of the vanga family.

children.name: Mystacornis crossleyi
children.description: A secretive, terrestrial vanga with olive-brown upperparts, a pale throat, and a long, slender bill, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It forages exclusively on the forest floor, probing leaf litter and moss for insects and other invertebrates. Its elusive behaviour and skulking habits make it one of the most difficult vangas to observe.

children.name: Schetba
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga containing the Rufous Vanga, a medium-sized, sexually dimorphic species endemic to Madagascar. The male is strikingly coloured with a rufous back and head, while the female is more subdued. It has a relatively stout bill and forages actively in the forest mid-storey.

children.name: Schetba rufa
children.description: A medium-sized vanga with the male showing a rufous head, back, and tail, black wings, and white underparts, endemic to the dry deciduous forests of western and northern Madagascar. The female has a brown head and back with a white throat. It forages actively in the canopy, often in pairs or small family groups.

children.name: Calicalicus
children.description: A genus of small, sexually dimorphic vangas endemic to Madagascar, with males sporting bright red or chestnut patches on the wings and tail. They are among the smallest members of the vanga family and are active, acrobatic foragers in the forest canopy. Their short, stout bills are adapted for gleaning insects from foliage rather than the specialised foraging of other vangas.

children.name: Calicalicus madagascariensis
children.description: A small vanga with the male a warm grey-brown above with a striking red tail and red wing patches, endemic to Madagascar's eastern rainforests. The female is duller, with buff underparts and less extensive red in the tail. It forages actively in the canopy, often in mixed-species flocks, feeding on insects and small fruits.

children.name: Oriolia
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga containing Bernier's Vanga, a striking black-and-white species endemic to Madagascar. It has a stout, slightly hooked bill and a distinctive white wing patch. It is a forest-dwelling insectivore that forages actively in the canopy, often in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Oriolia bernieri
children.description: A striking vanga with entirely black plumage in the male broken only by a bold white patch on the wing, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. The female is brown with streaked underparts. It forages actively in the canopy, gleaning insects from foliage, and is often encountered in mixed-species feeding flocks.

children.name: Xenopirostris
children.description: A genus of three medium-sized vangas endemic to Madagascar, characterised by a laterally compressed, blade-like bill that is unique among birds. This remarkable adaptation is used to peel bark and pry into crevices in search of prey. They inhabit dry forests and are among the most specialised foragers in the vanga family.

children.name: Xenopirostris polleni
children.description: A medium-sized vanga with a distinctive laterally compressed, blade-like bill, grey upperparts, and white underparts, endemic to the dry deciduous forests of western Madagascar. It uses its unique bill to pry bark and probe crevices for insect prey. It is secretive and poorly studied, inhabiting dense forest.

children.name: Xenopirostris xenopirostris
children.description: A striking vanga with a unique flattened bill, black-and-white plumage, and a bold black breast band, endemic to the spiny forests of southern Madagascar. Its specialised bill is used to extract prey from under bark and from the spines of Didierea plants. It is an agile forager, often hanging upside-down while probing for insects.

children.name: Xenopirostris damii
children.description: An endangered vanga with a unique compressed bill, grey head, white underparts, and black wings, endemic to the dry deciduous forests of northern Madagascar. It is one of the rarest vangas, with a restricted distribution and declining habitat. Its specialised foraging technique involves using its blade-like bill to peel bark from dead branches.

children.name: Falculea
children.description: A monotypic vanga genus containing the Sickle-billed Vanga, which possesses the most extreme bill morphology in the family: a long, slender, strongly decurved bill resembling a sickle. This highly specialised tool is used to probe deep into tree crevices and bark furrows for large insects. Endemic to Madagascar, it is the largest species in the vanga family.

children.name: Falculea palliata
children.description: A large, spectacular vanga with a long, slender, downward-curving bill that accounts for nearly half its head length, found in dry deciduous forests of western and southern Madagascar. It uses its extraordinary bill to extract insect prey from deep crevices in bark and dead wood. It is highly social, often foraging in noisy family groups.

children.name: Cyanolanius
children.description: A monotypic genus of strikingly coloured vanga with brilliant azure-blue upperparts that are unique among Malagasy passerines. It has a relatively slender, straight bill and is more of a canopy insectivore than other vangas. Its distribution extends beyond Madagascar to the Comoros Islands, unusual for the family, which is otherwise entirely restricted to Madagascar.

children.name: Cyanolanius madagascarinus
children.description: A stunning vanga with bright azure-blue upperparts, a black eye-stripe, and white underparts, found in the forests of northern and eastern Madagascar and the Comoros. It is an active, agile forager that gleans insects from leaves and branches in the canopy, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its clear, whistled song is a characteristic sound of the Malagasy forest canopy.

children.name: Euryceros
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the extraordinary Helmet Vanga, one of the most distinctive birds in Madagascar. It has a massive, deep, laterally compressed bill that is a vivid blue-grey and forms a prominent casque on the forehead. This remarkable adaptation is used to crush large insects and other prey. It is a large, robust vanga of eastern rainforests.

children.name: Euryceros prevostii
children.description: An extraordinary vanga with a massive, deep blue-grey bill that forms a prominent casque on the forehead, combined with black plumage and chestnut underparts, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It forages slowly and deliberately in the canopy, using its powerful bill to crush large insects, beetles, and even small vertebrates. Its deep, croaking calls echo through the forest.

children.name: Hypositta
children.description: A monotypic genus of small vanga containing the Nuthatch Vanga, which convergently resembles a nuthatch in both morphology and behaviour. It has a short, straight bill and strong feet, and is unique among vangas for its tree-creeping foraging style, spiralling up trunks and branches in search of insects. Endemic to Madagascar.

children.name: Hypositta corallirostris
children.description: A small, nuthatch-like vanga with blue-grey upperparts, a short tail, and a vivid coral-red bill, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It forages by creeping up tree trunks and along branches, probing bark for insects in the manner of a nuthatch. Its high-pitched calls and distinctive foraging style make it one of the most unusual members of the vanga family.

children.name: Newtonia
children.description: A genus of small, warbler-like vangas endemic to Madagascar, with relatively slender, pointed bills and active foraging styles. They were traditionally placed in the Old World warbler family Sylviidae before molecular studies revealed their true affinities within the vanga family. They are insectivorous birds of forest and scrub habitats.

children.name: Newtonia amphichroa
children.description: A small, sombre grey-brown vanga with a relatively long tail and a slender bill, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It forages actively in the understorey and mid-storey, gleaning insects from leaves and twigs. Its simple, thin, high-pitched song is a characteristic sound of the Malagasy forest interior.

children.name: Newtonia brunneicauda
children.description: A small, active warbler-like vanga with brown upperparts, a pale eyebrow, and a pale belly, widespread across Madagascar in forests, scrub, and secondary growth. It is one of the most common and adaptable Malagasy birds, constantly flicking its wings and tail as it forages for insects. Its song is a repetitive, high-pitched trill.

children.name: Newtonia archboldi
children.description: A small, pale grey-brown vanga with white underparts and a slender bill, endemic to the spiny forests and dry scrub of southern Madagascar. It forages actively in low vegetation, often in pairs, gleaning insects from leaves and branches. Its thin, insect-like calls reveal its presence in the arid bush.

children.name: Newtonia fanovanae
children.description: A small, elusive vanga with olive-brown upperparts and a distinctive rufous tail, endemic to the rainforests of eastern Madagascar. It was not scientifically described until 1990 and remains one of the least known Malagasy birds. It forages in the understorey and mid-storey, gleaning insects from foliage in dense forest.

children.name: Newtonia micrura
children.description: A very small vanga with short tail, found in dry forests of western Madagascar.

children.name: Newtonia larvata
children.description: A masked newtonia of the Comoro Islands.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus
children.description: Small vangas formerly placed in the thrush family.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus sharpei
children.description: A rock thrush from the forests of eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Tylas
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga with striking black-and-white plumage.

children.name: Tylas albicollis
children.description: A striking black-and-white vanga from eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Oryzoborus
children.description: A genus of seed-eating birds formerly placed in the tanager family.

children.name: Oryzoborus angolensis
children.description: A seed-finch from South America.

children.name: Tylas
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga.

children.name: Tylas albicollis
children.description: A striking black-and-white vanga from eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus
children.description: Small vangas from Madagascar.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus sharpei
children.description: A rock thrush from eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus
children.description: Small vangas from Madagascar.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus sharpei
children.description: A rock thrush from eastern Madagascar.

children.name: Pseudocossyphus bensoni
children.description: A rock thrush from Madagascar.

children.name: Tylas
children.description: A monotypic genus of vanga.

children.name: Tylas albicollis
children.description: A striking black-and-white vanga from eastern Madagascar.
